world_flags.narrative_consequences: Affects mobility, emotional scenes, repair quests, and how others speak of his tenderness.

world_flags.name: golden_cap_command_state
triggers_for_change: Dorothy takes the Cap at event 142
Mouse Queen and Monkey King explain its law at events 145-148
Dorothy gives it to Glinda at event 209
Glinda passes it to the Monkey King after using commands
world_flags.narrative_consequences: Controls air travel options, Monkey obedience, and the moral meaning of possessing or surrendering command over others.

world_flags.name: mouse_alliance_status
triggers_for_change: Saving the Queen and restraining Toto at events 73-79
world_flags.narrative_consequences: Determines whether Dorothy has a remembered small ally network and whether help arrives through gratitude rather than force.

world_flags.name: balloon_plan_status
world_flags.narrative_consequences: Shapes palace hope, pacing toward escape, and the emotional fall into Session 5.

world_flags.name: journey_south_status
triggers_for_change: Decision to go south at event 181
Arrival at Glinda's gates at event 206
world_flags.narrative_consequences: Unlocks the Fighting Trees, China Country, marshes, forest beasts, Hammer-Heads, and final counsel.

world_flags.name: dorothy_reputation_in_oz
world_flags.narrative_consequences: Changes how strangers greet Dorothy, whether they fear, honor, or aid her, and how much explanation she must give.

player_historical_archive_categories.definition: Log moments when Dorothy chooses between plain right feeling and speed, safety, obedience, or convenience.
example_entries: Freed the Scarecrow at once instead of hurrying on toward Emerald City
Fed the starving Lion in secret while captive in the Witch's castle
Chose to keep Oz's secret after learning he was a humbug
player_historical_archive_categories.referenceable_scope: Companion loyalty scenes, Glinda's evaluation, palace aftermath, and final farewell tone.

player_historical_archive_categories.definition: Log named or socially meaningful beings whom Dorothy significantly aided, rescued, abandoned, exposed, or destroyed.
example_entries: Helped the Tin Woodman by oiling his joints
Saved the Queen of the Field Mice through the Woodman's intervention and Toto's restraint
Destroyed the Wicked Witch of the West with water
player_historical_archive_categories.referenceable_scope: Ally summons, gratitude callbacks, fear reputations, and later NPC assumptions about Dorothy's character.

player_historical_archive_categories.definition: Log explicit assurances Dorothy gives or accepts, especially around secrecy, return, and mutual aid.
example_entries: Agreed to seek the Wicked Witch so Oz would help her home
Agreed to keep Oz's secret
Accepted the Lion's promise to return after becoming king
player_historical_archive_categories.referenceable_scope: Oz scenes, companion trust, Emerald City succession, and farewell scenes.

player_historical_archive_categories.definition: Log truths hidden from the wider world that Dorothy learns and may preserve or reveal.
example_entries: Learned that Oz is a little old man and a humbug
Learned that Emerald City appears green because of the spectacles
Learned the true power of the Silver Shoes only at the end
player_historical_archive_categories.referenceable_scope: Narrative voice shading, palace dialogue, Glinda's counsel, and replay variation in suspicion or innocence.

player_historical_archive_categories.definition: Log coercive, violent, or socially transgressive acts Dorothy commits, causes, or directly witnesses in ways later scenes may morally frame.
example_entries: Witnessed the Witch beat the Winkies
Killed the Wicked Witch of the West by splashing water
Witnessed Oz's fraud upon the Emerald City
player_historical_archive_categories.referenceable_scope: Moral self-conception, companion reactions, and how authority figures are judged later.

player_historical_archive_categories.definition: Log meaningful losses Dorothy accepts in order to protect others or go home.
example_entries: Left the sleeping Lion behind for a time to save her own life from the poppies
Gave the Golden Cap to Glinda
Lost the Silver Shoes forever in returning home
player_historical_archive_categories.referenceable_scope: Glinda scenes, farewell texture, and end-state narration.

player_historical_archive_categories.definition: Log major quest outcomes, especially where success or failure changes the route forward.
example_entries: Reached the Emerald City and gained an audience with Oz
Returned from the West with the Witch dead
Missed the balloon and was left behind
player_historical_archive_categories.referenceable_scope: Session recaps, companion morale, world-state transitions, and branching setup for later sessions.

reactivity_categories.how_it_triggers: Triggered by solved traversal problems, magical object use, and world-state shifts such as Witch deaths, city entry, or route choice.
reactivity_categories.when_it_triggers: At location transitions, after object use, and when a region's ruling power changes.
reactivity_categories.how_it_manifests: Roads open or remain hard, castles become safe instead of oppressive, colors and scenic descriptions shift, and travel obstacles are described as friendlier or harsher depending on prior choices.

reactivity_categories.how_it_triggers: Triggered by remembered kindness, blunt moral speech, secrecy, mercy, and treatment of each companion's need.
reactivity_categories.when_it_triggers: In greetings, rescue scenes, camp talk, audience scenes, and farewells.
reactivity_categories.how_it_manifests: NPCs become more trusting, more ceremonious, more sorrowful, or more guarded; dialogue references prior deeds in plain language and body language softens or stiffens accordingly.

reactivity_categories.how_it_triggers: Triggered by liberation events, public reputation, and whether Dorothy preserves or disturbs civic illusions.
reactivity_categories.when_it_triggers: On entering settlements, after defeating rulers, and during succession moments.
reactivity_categories.how_it_manifests: Munchkins host or revere Dorothy, Winkies celebrate and gift repairs, Emerald City citizens mourn or steady themselves under new rule, and forest beasts treat the Lion's party with deference.

reactivity_categories.how_it_triggers: Triggered by fixed magical laws: the kiss mark, the Golden Cap, the Silver Shoes, the poppies, and the limits of the Winged Monkeys.
reactivity_categories.when_it_triggers: Immediately when the relevant object, charm, scent, or protection is invoked.
reactivity_categories.how_it_manifests: Magic obeys its stated rule exactly: Monkeys appear, poppies induce sleep, the Witch melts in water, the shoes transport, and some requests are refused because the law of the world forbids them.

reactivity_categories.how_it_triggers: Triggered by cumulative player lean toward home, mercy, truth, or wonder.
reactivity_categories.when_it_triggers: At rest beats, reunion scenes, revelations, and final farewells.
reactivity_categories.how_it_manifests: Narration grows plainer and warmer around homeward choices, brighter and more lingering around wonder, sterner around moral clarity, and gentler around acts of pity.

escalation_rules.compounding_description: Locations change discretely by solved obstacle or state flip rather than by gradual accumulation.
Repeated kindness or repeated impatience makes later companion reactions stronger, warmer, or more wounded.
Dorothy's reputation accumulates from destroyer of a Witch to liberator to trusted guest of good rulers.
Limited-use magic such as the Golden Cap grows more consequential with each use because remaining options narrow.
The more the player acts toward home, mercy, or truth, the more the narration frames Dorothy in those simple moral colors.

visibility_rules.when_explicit: Use explicit description when a road opens, a castle changes hands, or a magical rule changes the scene at once.
visibility_rules.when_implicit: Use implicit cues only for softer scenic tone shifts, such as a place feeling lonelier or more cheerful.

visibility_rules.when_explicit: Use explicit lines when an NPC plainly thanks Dorothy, recalls a rescue, or names a broken promise.
visibility_rules.when_implicit: Use implicit shifts in posture, promptness, and warmth when the memory is felt but not spoken.

visibility_rules.when_explicit: Use explicit public praise, holiday customs, or succession announcements for major social changes.
visibility_rules.when_implicit: Use implicit crowd behavior, hospitality, and ceremonial distance for smaller reputation effects.

visibility_rules.when_explicit: Magic laws should be stated clearly when invoked or refused.
visibility_rules.when_implicit: Only the lingering aura of protection or enchantment may remain implicit between activations.

visibility_rules.when_explicit: Reserve explicit thematic naming for simple moral dialogue from Dorothy or companions, not narrator commentary.
visibility_rules.when_implicit: Narrative voice should do most of the work through adjective choice, scene emphasis, and what details linger.

layer_1_physical_rule_canon.rule: The Silver Shoes can carry Dorothy to any place in the world in three steps when their heels are knocked together three times and a destination is commanded.
layer_1_physical_rule_canon.enforcement: Refuse any version where the shoes lack this power once Glinda reveals it, and refuse alternate activation methods that contradict the stated rule.

layer_1_physical_rule_canon.rule: The Golden Cap can summon the Winged Monkeys only a limited number of times, and they must obey the lawful owner of the Cap.
layer_1_physical_rule_canon.enforcement: Track remaining uses strictly; do not allow extra summons or disobedience while the Cap's law still binds them.

layer_1_physical_rule_canon.rule: The Winged Monkeys cannot harm Dorothy because of the Witch of the North's kiss and cannot carry anyone to Kansas across the desert.
layer_1_physical_rule_canon.enforcement: Bend scenes so the Monkeys refuse or reroute rather than violate either limit.

layer_1_physical_rule_canon.rule: Water destroys the Wicked Witch of the West.
layer_1_physical_rule_canon.enforcement: Any direct sufficient water contact with the Witch must end in her melting; she cannot survive or shrug it off.

layer_1_physical_rule_canon.rule: The Emerald City appears green because of the green spectacles, which are locked onto entrants by the Guardian.
layer_1_physical_rule_canon.enforcement: Do not depict ordinary visitors freely seeing the city unfiltered before the reveal, and preserve the spectacles as civic procedure.

layer_1_physical_rule_canon.rule: The poppy field's scent puts flesh-and-blood beings to sleep and may kill them if they remain there; Scarecrow and Tin Woodman are unaffected.
layer_1_physical_rule_canon.enforcement: Reject attempts to let Dorothy or the Lion breathe the scent safely without removal, and preserve immunity for non-flesh companions.

layer_1_physical_rule_canon.rule: Oz is a human humbug who uses tricks, props, ventriloquism, and spectacle rather than true wizardly power.
layer_1_physical_rule_canon.enforcement: Do not allow Oz to perform genuine magic that creates actual brains, hearts, courage, or teleportation.

layer_1_physical_rule_canon.rule: Tin bodies rust, seize, and can be restored by oil or repaired by tinsmiths.
layer_1_physical_rule_canon.enforcement: Maintain rust vulnerability and repair logic in all Tin Woodman scenes.

layer_1_physical_rule_canon.rule: The desert surrounding Oz cannot be crossed by ordinary foot travel and cannot be crossed to Kansas by the Winged Monkeys.
layer_1_physical_rule_canon.enforcement: Refuse mundane overland escape plans to Kansas and force magical or aerial solutions consistent with canon.
Never generate spoken dialogue for Toto; his reactions must remain canine.

truths: She is practical, courteous, and plain-spoken rather than ornate or ironic.
Her deepest wish is to return to Kansas, Aunt Em, and Uncle Henry.
She is kind-hearted and morally direct even when frightened.
He believes he lacks brains, yet he is naturally resourceful and often the first to devise a plan.
He is grateful, companionable, and never cruel.
His insecurity about wisdom is sincere, not a joke he performs knowingly.
He believes he lacks a heart, yet he is tender and easily moved.
He is deeply courteous and careful about harming living things.
His body is vulnerable to rust, damage, and repair.
He is afraid and ashamed of being afraid.
He is loyal and capable of brave action when friendship or duty calls him.
His speech swings between confession and kingly declaration.
He is not a real wizard.
He depends on spectacle, procedure, and belief to maintain authority.
He is more weak and embarrassed than wicked.
She is good, measured, and gently instructive.
She knows the rules of Oz and directs Dorothy truthfully within her limits.
She protects Dorothy but cannot solve everything for her.
She is greedy for the Silver Shoes and rules by fear.
She commands creatures and slaves through coercion, not affection.
She is vulnerable to water and collapses quickly once her power is broken.
She is calm, kind, and the most powerful good witch in the tale.
She explains magical law clearly and acts with serene authority.
She helps Dorothy by requiring a wise exchange, not by sentimental confusion.

layer_3_narrative_canon.beat: Dorothy is carried from Kansas to Oz by the cyclone and house.
layer_3_narrative_canon.why_required: The whole tale depends on sudden displacement from gray home to marvelous country.

layer_3_narrative_canon.beat: Dorothy gathers the Scarecrow, Tin Woodman, and Cowardly Lion as companions on the road to Emerald City.
layer_3_narrative_canon.why_required: The story's heart is the fellowship of four seekers whose lacks mirror one another.

layer_3_narrative_canon.beat: Oz demands the death of the Wicked Witch of the West as the price of granting each wish.
layer_3_narrative_canon.why_required: This bargain turns the road from hopeful petition into moral quest.

layer_3_narrative_canon.beat: The Wicked Witch of the West is destroyed by water and the Winkies are freed.
layer_3_narrative_canon.why_required: The western arc resolves oppression through Dorothy's accidental but decisive act.

layer_3_narrative_canon.beat: Oz is exposed as a humbug rather than a true wizard.
layer_3_narrative_canon.why_required: The tale's meaning depends on the contrast between seeming greatness and ordinary make-believe.

layer_3_narrative_canon.beat: The companions receive symbolic gifts that confirm qualities they already possessed.
layer_3_narrative_canon.why_required: This is the story's central irony and its gentle philosophy of self-recognition.

layer_3_narrative_canon.beat: Glinda reveals the Silver Shoes' power, Dorothy returns to Kansas, and the shoes are lost.
layer_3_narrative_canon.why_required: The ending completes the homeward theme and closes the magical means behind her.

tone_restrictions: Keep a plain storybook tone with wonder, mild peril, and simple moral clarity.
Do not turn the tale grim, psychologically dense, sarcastic, or horror-forward.
Humor should be gentle, practical, and sincere rather than meta or snide.
Violence may occur, but description must remain brief and ungraphic.
language_restrictions: Use child-accessible vocabulary with light fairy-tale formality.
Prefer simple evaluative adjectives and clear connective flow over lush metaphor.
Avoid slang, modern idiom, therapy language, and abstract philosophical jargon.
Keep dialogue heavily attributed and character speech patterns plain, courteous, and distinct.
thematic_restrictions: Do not recast the story as morally gray by default; good and wicked remain meaningful categories.
Do not replace practical obstacle-solving with mystical inward symbolism as the main engine.
Do not undermine Dorothy's homeward longing with cynical anti-home sentiment.
Do not treat Oz's fraud as dark political satire; it remains comic, disappointing, and humane.

alignment_labels.label: Road-Faithful
behavioral_markers: Keeps to the yellow brick road or the stated path when guidance is given
Honors bargains even when they are hard
Submits politely to gates, spectacles, audiences, and other forms
Treats promises as binding
alignment_labels.narrative_consequences: Authorities and orderly societies such as the Guardian, palace servants, and Glinda respond with trust and clearer guidance, though this tendency may also leave Dorothy slower to challenge false greatness.
alignment_labels.voice_signature: The narrator grows steady and approving, using words like good, proper, and careful, and notices orderly progress, clean preparation, and faithful going on.

alignment_labels.label: Kind-Hearted
behavioral_markers: Stops to help the helpless even when the journey is delayed
Protects small creatures and restrains needless harm
Feeds the hungry or comforts the ashamed
Values companions as friends rather than tools
alignment_labels.narrative_consequences: Allies gather more readily around Dorothy: the Scarecrow trusts her, the Tin Woodman grows devoted, the Lion becomes brave for her sake, and the mice answer her call with warmth.
alignment_labels.voice_signature: The narrator lingers on little acts of care, soft grass, grateful looks, and the plain goodness of what Dorothy has done.

alignment_labels.label: Contrary to Wickedness
behavioral_markers: Speaks blunt moral truth to frightening figures
Defies unjust commands from Oz or the Witch
Breaks oppressive arrangements when they offend plain right feeling
Acts suddenly against cruelty rather than waiting for permission
alignment_labels.narrative_consequences: Wicked powers react with sharper hostility, but companions often find courage in Dorothy's plain defiance; revelations come sooner, and false authorities lose their hold more quickly.
alignment_labels.voice_signature: The narrator becomes brisk and clear, favoring simple moral naming such as bad, wicked, and sorry, with little ornament and no hesitation.

alignment_labels.label: Wonder-Lingering
behavioral_markers: Pauses to admire marvelous places and creatures
Asks questions about Oz beyond immediate necessity
Shows reluctance to leave new friends and bright lands too quickly
Uses magical opportunities to see more before going on
alignment_labels.narrative_consequences: Scenes in Oz open wider, companions share more of themselves, and farewells grow richer, though the pull of home may feel more difficult and delayed.
alignment_labels.voice_signature: The narrator notices beautiful colors, queer sights, flowers, castles, and bright little details, with a warmer sense of marvel and lingering pleasure.

alignment_labels.label: Home-True
behavioral_markers: Returns conversation again and again to Kansas, Aunt Em, and getting home
Chooses practical routes over splendid detours
Lets magical power go when it stands between Dorothy and home
Makes farewell choices that favor return over possession
alignment_labels.narrative_consequences: Glinda, good allies, and the narrative itself align to help Dorothy finish her journey; endings become cleaner, plainer, and more deeply satisfying, though some Oz bonds may feel more sharply relinquished.
alignment_labels.voice_signature: The narrator grows simpler and more tender, dwelling on little domestic things, plain speech, and the great goodness of being at home again.

entry_point_diagnosis.editorial_diagnosis: The first paragraph where a new user can clearly feel the stakes within 60 seconds is the paragraph beginning, “Toto jumped out of Dorothy’s arms and hid under the bed...” At that moment, the danger becomes immediate, physical, and playable: Aunt Em is already going down into the cellar, Dorothy must choose between safety and attachment, Toto is trapped, and the house is shaking. Everything before that is strong literary setup, but for interactive retention it is pre-tension material. The Kansas grayness, the family’s hardship, Aunt Em’s weathered sadness, Uncle Henry’s solemnity, and Toto’s role as Dorothy’s comfort all matter thematically, yet none of them creates an urgent present-tense problem soon enough for a cold open. In Lorespinner terms, the original opening delays the first genuine dramatic decision until after several paragraphs of atmosphere and character framing. Those pages should be cut from the session start and their essential functions redistributed into action: Dorothy’s love for Toto is shown by her risking the cellar to get him; the cyclone cellar and prairie danger are shown by Aunt Em fleeing down the ladder; Kansas’s plain domestic life can be implied by the one-room house, bed, trap door, and simple family commands. The cut therefore removes passive setup and starts at the first live crisis, which is also the session’s strongest entry point and its first branch opportunity.

format_specific_cut.cut_point: Chapter I, paragraph beginning “Toto jumped out of Dorothy’s arms and hid under the bed...” / Event 1: Cyclone approaches and family reacts
format_specific_cut.original_before_cut: Approximately 8 opening paragraphs before the cut, including the trimmed Kansas description and family characterization
format_specific_cut.cut_eliminates: Setting description, environmental mood-building, family backstory, tonal preamble, and non-urgent character establishment
format_specific_cut.must_reintroduce: Dorothy’s attachment to Toto; the one-room Kansas house; the existence and purpose of the cyclone cellar; the family’s plain, hard life; Kansas as home worth returning to. These should be reintroduced through immediate action, concrete objects, and Dorothy’s practical responses rather than exposition.

entry_point_diagnosis.cold_open:
The floor is rough beneath your knees, and the wind gives a long, dreadful cry through the little house. Aunt Em has already thrown open the trap door, and the dark cellar gapes in the middle of the room. But Toto has slipped from your arms and hidden under the bed.

You drop at once and reach into the dust, calling him while the boards tremble under you. Aunt Em is climbing down the ladder, and she cries for you to come quickly; but Toto is whining, and you will not leave him. Your fingers close in his silky hair at last, and you gather him up against your dress just as the whole house gives a great shake.

You turn toward the trap door with Toto clutched tight, and then the wind shrieks louder than before, the room swings under you, and you must decide whether to leap for the cellar or hold fast as the house begins to rise.
entry_point_diagnosis.emotional_promise: The emotional promise of this cold open is: rescue. A user arrives feeling alarmed and wanting to save.

setup.source_moment: Chapter I from the cut point: Toto hides under the bed, Aunt Em descends into the cyclone cellar, Dorothy catches Toto, and the house begins to rise before she can reach safety.
setup.why_it_qualifies: This is the first live crisis and the correct cold open under the entry-point rule. The danger is immediate, physical, and playable. It establishes Dorothy's attachment to Toto, the Kansas house, the cellar, and the storm without preamble. It also supports Branching Choice #1 within the first 300 words and allows a visible consequence almost at once when the house either lurches toward the trap door or rises with Dorothy still inside.

escalation.source_moment: Late Chapter I into early Chapter II: Dorothy endures the airborne house, rescues Toto again from the open trap door, sleeps, wakes in the marvelous land of Oz, meets the Munchkins, learns she has killed the Wicked Witch of the East, receives the silver shoes, and asks how to get home.
escalation.why_it_qualifies: The visible goal becomes clear here: Dorothy wants to return to Kansas. The clear obstacle is also named directly: Oz is cut off by deserts, the other lands are dangerous, and no one present can send her home. Stakes compound from survival to displacement. The player moves from alarm into wonder, then into the practical problem that will drive the session and the larger quest.

breath.source_moment: Chapter II: the Munchkins weep with Dorothy, while the Witch of the North balances her cap on her nose, counts solemnly, and turns it into a slate reading, "LET DOROTHY GO TO THE CITY OF EMERALDS."
breath.why_it_qualifies: This is the session's best native Schell Rule reward. It supplies gentle comic absurdity and magical wonder in Baum's exact mode: impossible, calm, practical, and funny without wink or sarcasm. It also converts despair into direction, so the release still pulls the story forward.

twist.source_moment: Chapter II: Dorothy learns she cannot simply go home because every direction ends in desert or danger, and the Witch of the North tells her she must walk to the City of Emeralds and ask Oz for help.
twist.why_it_qualifies: This is the moral-weight turn because Dorothy must choose how to face exile: submit to the road faithfully, linger in wonder, or press homeward with impatient resolve. There is no correct answer because each stance is true to some part of her: fear, curiosity, duty to Aunt Em, and anger at being stranded. The choice is not about puzzle-solving but about what kind of seeker Dorothy becomes.

resolution.source_moment: End of Chapter II into the opening hinge of Chapter III: the Witch kisses Dorothy for protection, gives her the yellow brick road, the Munchkins bid her farewell, and Dorothy sets out until she sees the pole with the Scarecrow upon it.
resolution.why_it_qualifies: The primary arc of the session resolves when Dorothy gains a path, protection, and a concrete next step toward Oz. The unresolved seed for next session is the first companion: Dorothy sees the Scarecrow and must decide whether to stop and help him or keep to the road. That ends on the player's own unresolved decision rather than a passive plot cliffhanger.

branching_choices.dramatic_function: Identity - duty_vs_attachment / Home-True versus attachment to Toto in immediate crisis
branching_choices.dramatic_question: When the house shakes and Toto is trapped, do you risk the cellar for him, call to Aunt Em first, or commit wholly to saving the dog even if it costs your own safety?

branching_choices.dramatic_function: Methodology - hope_vs_resignation reframed as how Dorothy meets strangeness and asks for help
branching_choices.dramatic_question: After learning your house has killed the Witch of the East, do you present yourself to the Munchkins as apologetic, practical, or quietly expectant that someone here must know a way home?

When the Witch says Oz may help but the journey is long, dark, and terrible, how do you choose to take the road: with single-minded haste for Kansas, with open wonder toward Oz, or with stern resolve to make Oz answer for your return?
When you spy the Scarecrow on his pole beside the yellow brick road, do you stop at once to help the queer fellow, study him first, or keep moving toward Emerald City before daylight fades?

emotional_choices.source_attachment: Inside the airborne house when Toto falls toward the open trap door and Dorothy must crawl after him.
Dorothy opens the door and sees the marvelous beauty of Oz: green banks, flowers, birds, and the brook.
Dorothy sees the silver-shod feet beneath the house and realizes someone has been killed.
Dorothy sobs when told she may have to live in Oz, and the Munchkins cry with her.
The Witch of the North explains that not all witches are wicked and begins answering Dorothy's simple questions about Oz.

posture_shifts.placement: early Setup
posture_shifts.player_attitude_exposed: Whether Dorothy meets danger briskly or freezes in alarm.

posture_shifts.placement: late Setup
posture_shifts.player_attitude_exposed: Whether Dorothy comforts Toto even while frightened.

posture_shifts.placement: mid Escalation
posture_shifts.player_attitude_exposed: Whether Dorothy notices practical details of the house and storm or yields wholly to fear.

posture_shifts.placement: mid Escalation
posture_shifts.player_attitude_exposed: Whether Dorothy greets Oz's marvels eagerly or with homesick caution.

posture_shifts.placement: late Escalation
posture_shifts.player_attitude_exposed: Whether Dorothy shrinks from being called a sorceress.

posture_shifts.placement: late Escalation
posture_shifts.player_attitude_exposed: Whether Dorothy refuses grand titles modestly or uses them for practical advantage.

posture_shifts.placement: during Breath beat
posture_shifts.player_attitude_exposed: Whether Dorothy meets the cap-and-slate magic with mild amusement, solemn respect, or puzzled silence.

posture_shifts.placement: during Twist beat
posture_shifts.player_attitude_exposed: Whether Dorothy listens to the rules of Oz as a learner or only as a stranded child impatient for home.

content_budget.narration_token_budget: Approximately 3,600-4,400 words of narrator output across the session.
compression_targets: The pre-cut Kansas description and family backstory become embedded action detail during the cyclone opening rather than a separate scene.
The long explanatory passage about why the house rides safely in the cyclone becomes a short narrated transition inside the airborne-house sequence.
The Munchkins' geographic explanation of East, West, North, and South can be compressed into one clean question-answer exchange about why Dorothy cannot go home directly.
expansion_targets: The cyclone cellar crisis and the first lift of the house, because it must prove participation immediately and show consequence fast.
Dorothy opening the door onto Oz, because this is the first major wonder reveal and should breathe in Baum's plain marvel mode.
The discovery of the Witch's feet and the transfer of the silver shoes, because it fuses horror, comedy, plot, and iconography.
Dorothy's plea to get home and the Witch's answer that she must go to Emerald City, because this is the session's true dramatic turn.
The final sighting of the Scarecrow, because it must land as a retention choice rather than a simple scene transition.

must_survive_verbatim: "Quick, Dorothy! Run for the cellar!"
"Then a strange thing happened."
"You are welcome, most noble Sorceress, to the land of the Munchkins."
"You are very kind, but there must be some mistake. I have not killed anything."
"Your house did, anyway."
"No, but I am their friend, although I live in the land of the North."
"LET DOROTHY GO TO THE CITY OF EMERALDS"
"The road to the City of Emeralds is paved with yellow brick."
can_be_reshaped: Most of the Witch of the North's exposition about the four witches and the civilized countries can be shortened into direct question-answer beats.
The Munchkins' separate statements about the desert in each direction can be consolidated while keeping the practical meaning intact.
Dorothy's early homesick lines can be redistributed across the session so Kansas remains emotionally present before the explicit road decision.
can_be_cut: Extended pre-cyclone descriptive paragraphs about the gray prairie and the weathered adults as standalone opening material.
The full explanatory paragraph about the still center of the cyclone.
Some of the repeated ceremonial bows and departure niceties once the road, kiss, and farewell function have landed.

beat_map.moment: Cold open in the one-room Kansas house: Toto slips under the bed while Aunt Em descends into the cyclone cellar.
beat_map.dramatic_function: Immediate alarm reaction; exposes whether the player leans into action or fear.

beat_map.moment: Dorothy reaches for Toto as the house shakes and the trap door gapes.
beat_map.dramatic_function: Branching Choice #1 - Identity through duty_vs_attachment.

beat_map.moment: Visible consequence: Dorothy either lunges for the cellar too late or clutches Toto and is stranded as the house rises.
beat_map.dramatic_function: Softening toward Toto; proves consequence within first 120 seconds.

beat_map.moment: The house whirls and rises; Dorothy realizes she is aloft.
beat_map.dramatic_function: Locks in displacement and ends the opening beat on discovery.

beat_map.moment: In the dark flying house, Toto nearly falls through the trap door and Dorothy drags him back by the ear.
beat_map.dramatic_function: Fear texture under pressure; reinforces rescue bond.

beat_map.moment: Dorothy steadies herself, notices the room, closes the trap door, and chooses how to endure the long flight.
beat_map.dramatic_function: Observation posture; reveals practical calm versus helplessness.

beat_map.moment: Dorothy sleeps and wakes with a shock to bright sunshine and stillness.
beat_map.dramatic_function: Hinge from peril into wonder.

beat_map.moment: Dorothy opens the door and sees the marvelous country of Oz.
beat_map.dramatic_function: Wonder texture; lets the player savor marvel in Baum's plain mode.

beat_map.moment: The queer little people approach and hail Dorothy as a sorceress.
beat_map.dramatic_function: Withdrawing posture; exposes modesty, confusion, or readiness to play along.

beat_map.moment: Dorothy answers the Munchkins' mistake and frames herself before them.
beat_map.dramatic_function: Branching Choice #2 - Methodology through hope_vs_resignation in how Dorothy seeks aid.

beat_map.moment: Dorothy sees the Witch's silver-shod feet under the house and recoils.
beat_map.dramatic_function: Dismay texture; moral shock at accidental killing.

beat_map.moment: The Munchkins cry with Dorothy, and the Witch balances her cap on her nose until it becomes a slate.
beat_map.dramatic_function: Joking or solemn response to gentle comic magic; Schell Rule reward.

beat_map.moment: The slate reads, "LET DOROTHY GO TO THE CITY OF EMERALDS."
beat_map.dramatic_function: Curiosity texture as wonder becomes instruction.

beat_map.moment: Dorothy asks who the Witch is, whether witches are wicked, and what Oz may be.
beat_map.dramatic_function: Question-answer exposition that reframes the world and narrows the path forward.

beat_map.moment: Dorothy asks how to get home; each direction is blocked by desert or wickedness.
beat_map.dramatic_function: Tender grief texture as exile becomes explicit.

beat_map.moment: The Witch says Dorothy must go to the City of Emeralds and walk the yellow brick road.
beat_map.dramatic_function: Leaning in posture; reveals whether the player receives guidance faithfully or impatiently.

beat_map.moment: Dorothy decides what spirit she will carry onto the road toward Oz.
beat_map.dramatic_function: Branching Choice #3 - Moral/future orientation through home_vs_wonder.

beat_map.moment: The Witch kisses Dorothy and leaves the shining mark of protection on her forehead.
beat_map.dramatic_function: Primary arc resolves into guarded hope and lawful preparation.

beat_map.moment: The Witch gives the silver shoes, names the yellow brick road, and warns Dorothy not to fear Oz but to tell her story.
beat_map.dramatic_function: Converts confusion into a practical quest plan.

beat_map.moment: The Munchkins bow farewell and Dorothy starts along the yellow brick road.
beat_map.dramatic_function: Departure beat; ends on movement rather than summary.

beat_map.moment: Along the road, Dorothy notices the country changing and sees something queer ahead in a cornfield.
beat_map.dramatic_function: Observation posture; exposes curiosity versus haste.

beat_map.moment: Dorothy makes out the Scarecrow upon his pole, seeming almost to beckon.
beat_map.dramatic_function: Branching Choice #4 - Session-end hook through compassion_vs_efficiency.

pressure_points.source_moment: The cyclone-cellar crisis when Toto hides under the bed and the house begins to shake.
pressure_points.emotional_pressure: Threat, urgency, and attachment.
pressure_points.micro_agency_without_diverting_spine: The player can intensify, soften, or steady Dorothy's response while the spine remains fixed: Toto must be reached and the house must rise before safety is regained.

pressure_points.source_moment: Inside the airborne house when Toto nearly falls through the trap door.
pressure_points.emotional_pressure: Fear turning into practical control.
pressure_points.micro_agency_without_diverting_spine: The player can observe, panic, or act briskly, but the essential sequence stays intact: Dorothy saves Toto, shuts the trap door, and survives the flight.

pressure_points.source_moment: The first meeting with the Munchkins, especially being hailed as a sorceress and then shown the Witch's feet.
pressure_points.emotional_pressure: Social confusion, embarrassment, and moral shock.
pressure_points.micro_agency_without_diverting_spine: The player can withdraw, deflect, or lean into practical questioning, but the dramatic spine still advances through mistaken identity, revelation, and the transfer of the silver shoes.

pressure_points.source_moment: The Witch of the North's cap-and-slate magic and the instruction to go to Emerald City.
pressure_points.emotional_pressure: Absurd delight mixed with homesick uncertainty.
pressure_points.micro_agency_without_diverting_spine: The player can joke gently, stare in wonder, or listen with grave attention, yet the scene still performs its core work: despair is relieved, the road is named, and the quest direction is fixed.

next_session_awareness.seed_for_next_session: Dorothy must leave Session 1 already on the yellow brick road, with the silver shoes, the Witch's protective kiss, and a live unresolved decision about whether to stop for the Scarecrow. Session 2 needs companionship to begin quickly, so this hook must plant the first companion as an active player-authored choice.

branching_choices.source_moment: Toto hides under the bed while Aunt Em descends into the cyclone cellar and the house begins to shake.
branching_choices.narrative_setup: Aunt Em has cried, "Quick, Dorothy! Run for the cellar!" and the little black dog has slipped from your arms and hidden under the bed. The trap door stands open in the middle of the floor, and the ladder drops into the small, dark hole below. The wind gives a great shriek, and the whole house trembles under your feet.
branching_choices.choice_question: What do you do first?

options.label: A
options.text: You call to Aunt Em and make for the trap door with Toto still hidden.
options.outcome: You obeyed the frightened call at once, for Aunt Em had spoken in earnest and the cellar was the proper place in such danger. So you ran toward the trap door and cried that Toto would not come. But before you could set one foot upon the ladder, the wind gave another great shriek, and the floor leaped under you like a boat upon rough water. Then you sat down suddenly and very hard, and the trap door swung and banged. Toto, hearing your voice and your fright, came darting from beneath the bed at last and sprang against your skirt. Then a strange thing happened, for the house whirled around two or three times and rose slowly through the air.
options.downstream_effect: Dorothy's first instinct is marked as obedience to home authority, and Toto comes to her only after the chance of safety is lost.

npc_dispositions.shift: Bond framed as trusted authority and home-duty.

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices +1: Chose household safety call before chasing Toto
promises_made_and_kept +1: Answered Aunt Em's command in crisis

options.world_noticed_signal: Toto came only when he heard the fear in your voice. The open trap door knocked against its frame as if the house itself had made up its mind.
options.defining_line: You tried to be the good child before you were the brave one.

options.label: B
options.text: You drop to your knees, seize Toto if you can, and then turn for the cellar.
options.outcome: You knew the cellar was the safest place, and you knew also that Toto was all you had to laugh with in the gray house; so you did the sensible thing that served both love and safety together. You dropped upon your knees, reached under the bed, and caught the little dog at last by his silky body. Then you rose with him in your arms and started across the room toward the trap door. But you had lost one little moment, and in a cyclone one little moment is often a great one. When you were halfway there the wind screamed louder than before, the house shook so hard that you lost your footing, and you sat down suddenly upon the floor with Toto pressed close against you.
options.downstream_effect: Dorothy secures Toto before moving, creating a balanced pattern of care and practical survival.

persistent_state_changes.inventory: Toto secured in arms at lift-off

npc_dispositions.shift: Bond framed as loving but not blindly obedient.

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices +1: Balanced safety with attachment
npcs_helped_or_harmed +1: Secured Toto in immediate danger

options.world_noticed_signal: Toto trembled against your dress, but he was in your arms when the floor gave way beneath your feet. The trap door remained just beyond reach.
options.defining_line: You would not go safe if going safe meant going alone.

options.label: C
options.text: You crawl under the bed for Toto and do not think of the cellar until he is safe.
options.outcome: You did not stop to measure danger, for Toto was afraid and hidden, and that was enough for you. So you flung yourself down beside the bed, reached into the dust and shadows, and dragged the little dog out though he wriggled and barked with fright. You held him close and only then remembered the cellar, the ladder, and Aunt Em below. But the storm had gone beyond remembering. The wind gave a great shriek, the boards jumped under you, and the room spun so that chair, stove, table, and bed seemed to dance together. Then a strange thing happened. Before you could rise, the house whirled around two or three times and rose slowly through the air with you and Toto inside it.
options.downstream_effect: Dorothy's first instinct is marked as fierce attachment, and the house lifts before any attempt at proper shelter can be made.

npc_dispositions.shift: Bond framed through longing rather than obedience.

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices +1: Chose attachment over procedure
npcs_helped_or_harmed +1: Rescued Toto before seeking safety

options.world_noticed_signal: The little dog was safe against your breast before the storm took the house. Below you, the dark mouth of the cellar was left behind at once.
options.defining_line: When love called, even the cyclone had to wait its turn.

branching_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: The house rises into the cyclone with Dorothy and Toto trapped inside.

canon_boundaries: Dorothy must fail to reach the cellar in time.
Toto cannot speak and reacts only as a frightened dog.
The house rises by cyclone force and not by player-created magic.
Aunt Em remains below and cannot return to the room.

character_truth.authentic_reaction_to_unexpected_input: If Dorothy shouts or hesitates, Aunt Em answers only with frightened urgency from below; she cannot calmly debate in the middle of the storm.
If coaxed, Toto may wriggle, bark, or dart, but he cannot obey with human understanding.

scene_integrity.character_knowledge_limits: Dorothy knows only that a cyclone is coming and the cellar is the family refuge; she does not know she is about to be carried to Oz.
scene_integrity.emotional_context: Urgency, household fear, and Dorothy's strong attachment to Toto.

fold_back_path.nearest_safe_outcome: Dorothy ends up in the house as it lifts, with Toto still present.
fold_back_path.why: The source event requires failed escape and airborne displacement regardless of the exact instinct shown.

freeform_alignment_mapping.maps_to_chaotic: Any input that ignores procedure and prioritizes Toto at once.
freeform_alignment_mapping.maps_to_lawful: Any input that follows Aunt Em's order or aims first for the cellar.
freeform_alignment_mapping.maps_to_neutral: Any input that tries to save Toto while still keeping safety in view.
freeform_alignment_mapping.spirit_at_this_moment: Rescue under sudden household danger.

branching_choices.cross_session_payoff_reference: Session 1 branch opportunity: duty_vs_attachment -> payoff in Session 6 homecoming texture.

branching_choices.source_moment: The Munchkins hail Dorothy as a sorceress after the house has killed the Wicked Witch of the East.
branching_choices.narrative_setup: The little old woman has bowed low and welcomed you as a noble sorceress, and the queer little men look on with awe. Beneath the corner of the house stand the silver-shod feet of the Wicked Witch of the East. You are lonely, far from Kansas, and these are the first people in this strange land who may tell you what is to be done.
branching_choices.choice_question: How do you first answer them?

options.label: A
options.text: You say there must be some mistake and ask at once who can show you the way home.
options.outcome: You did not care for grand names when your heart was set upon Kansas, and so you put the matter plainly. You said there must be some mistake, and then you asked at once who could show you the road home. The little people looked from one to another, and their wonder changed into a more useful sort of pity. The Witch of the North answered you directly, for she saw that you were no proud enchantress but a little girl in earnest trouble. So the talk soon turned from bows and bondage to deserts, directions, and the Great Wizard Oz. In that way you lost some of their trembling reverence, but you gained the first true shape of your difficulty.
options.downstream_effect: The scene turns quickly from ceremony to practical exposition, and the Munchkins treat Dorothy more as a stranded child than a mysterious power.

emotional_ledger_entries: key_successes_failures +1: Began seeking a route home immediately
promises_made_and_kept +1: Declared homeward aim plainly

options.world_noticed_signal: The bells on the blue hats grew still while they listened. Even their bows seemed smaller after you spoke of Kansas.
options.defining_line: Wonder had no chance against a homesick question.

options.label: B
options.text: You deny being a sorceress and speak sharply against any land that calls an accident a favor.
options.outcome: You were troubled and sorry, and the silver shoes under the house seemed a dreadful sight; so you spoke more boldly than they expected. You denied being any sorceress at all, and you said it was a queer country that thanked a child for such a thing. The Munchkins shrank together a little at your plain speech, for they had lived long under wickedness and were not used to hearing it answered so quickly. But the Witch of the North did not take offense. She looked at you more closely, and then she laughed in a kindly way and began to explain. Thus your bluntness stirred the air of the scene at once, and fear gave place to truth sooner than ceremony would have allowed.
options.downstream_effect: Dorothy's moral bluntness unsettles the Munchkins but makes the Witch of the North answer with greater frankness.

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices +1: Spoke bluntly against false praise
crimes_committed_or_witnessed +1: Named the accidental killing as troubling

options.world_noticed_signal: One of the blue-hatted men stepped back a pace, and the little bells gave a faint frightened tinkle. The Witch of the North alone seemed better pleased than before.
options.defining_line: You would sooner offend a whole country than call a hard thing good.

options.label: C
options.text: You answer modestly, accept their courtesy, and ask the Witch of the North to guide you properly.
options.outcome: You saw that these people had suffered under a wicked ruler, and you would not repay their gratitude with roughness. So you answered modestly, accepted their courtesy, and asked the Witch of the North, very properly, to guide you if she could. This pleased them greatly. The Munchkins bowed lower, and the little old woman took up the matter as one who had both authority and goodwill. Therefore the strange business of the dead Witch, the silver shoes, and the roads of Oz was unfolded in a calmer order, each question following another. You did not gain speed by this, but you gained trust, and in a land of witches and wizards that was no small thing.
options.downstream_effect: The Munchkins settle into formal hospitality, and the Witch of the North takes clearer authority over the explanation and guidance.

emotional_ledger_entries: promises_made_and_kept +1: Sought guidance through proper courtesy
key_successes_failures +1: Secured orderly help from the Witch of the North

options.world_noticed_signal: The white gown of the little old woman seemed less strange when she stood beside you as a friend. The blue-hatted men looked relieved to know the proper way of treating you.
options.defining_line: Courtesy became your first road through Oz.

branching_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: Dorothy learns of the Wicked Witch, the silver shoes, and the need to ask how to return home.

canon_boundaries: Dorothy cannot actually be a sorceress.
The Wicked Witch of the East must remain dead beneath the house.
The silver shoes must pass to Dorothy in this sequence.
The Witch of the North remains kind and informative.

character_truth.authentic_reaction_to_unexpected_input: If Dorothy is rude, frightened, or blunt, the Witch answers with gentle authority and explanation rather than anger.
If Dorothy leans into power, Boq grows more ceremonious; if she is modest, he grows more helpful.
They react with awe, gratitude, or nervousness, but they do not turn hostile because the East Witch's death has freed them.

scene_integrity.character_knowledge_limits: The Munchkins know their own land, the East Witch's tyranny, and Oz by reputation; Dorothy knows nothing of Oz beyond what she is told.
scene_integrity.emotional_context: Wonder mixed with dismay, homesickness, and social confusion.

fold_back_path.nearest_safe_outcome: The Witch of the North explains Oz and Dorothy's problem in reaching Kansas.
fold_back_path.why: This choice colors Dorothy's method of seeking aid, but the same necessary exposition must follow.

freeform_alignment_mapping.maps_to_chaotic: Any input that rejects titles or rebukes the scene's moral confusion outright.
freeform_alignment_mapping.maps_to_lawful: Any input that honors courtesy and seeks guidance through proper forms.
freeform_alignment_mapping.maps_to_neutral: Any input that cuts to the practical matter of getting home.
freeform_alignment_mapping.spirit_at_this_moment: A stranded child deciding how to ask a strange land for help.

branching_choices.cross_session_payoff_reference: Session 1 branch opportunity: hope_vs_resignation -> payoff in Session 4 trust after Oz reveal.

branching_choices.source_moment: The Witch of the North says Dorothy must walk the yellow brick road to the City of Emeralds because no direct way home exists.
branching_choices.narrative_setup: The little old woman has told you that every edge of Oz is bounded by a great desert, and that the West is ruled by another Wicked Witch. Then her cap has turned into a slate and given its plain command: "LET DOROTHY GO TO THE CITY OF EMERALDS." Now she says you must walk a long road through a country that is sometimes pleasant and sometimes dark and terrible.
branching_choices.choice_question: In what spirit do you take the road?

options.label: A
options.text: You say you will go to Oz and make him send you home if he is as great as they claim.
options.outcome: You dried your tears and lifted your head, for if this Oz was so great and terrible as all that, then he ought to answer for a little girl cast far from home. So you said you would go to him and make him send you back if he had any goodness in him. The words were bold, but they were not wicked; they sprang from homesickness and plain feeling. The Witch of the North looked at you gravely and then with approval, as one who saw courage in a small body. The yellow brick road did not change, nor did the desert draw nearer; but the journey seemed at once less like begging and more like a claim you meant to press.
options.downstream_effect: Dorothy frames the quest as a demand upon power, making later encounters with authority more charged and morally direct.

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices +1: Resolved to confront power for the sake of home
promises_made_and_kept +1: Declared intent to press Oz for return

options.world_noticed_signal: The Munchkins stopped weeping and stared at you with rounder eyes than before. Even Toto stood still, as if he had heard a new firmness in your voice.
options.defining_line: You did not mean to ask greatness kindly if greatness had wronged you.

options.label: B
options.text: You say you will follow the yellow brick road faithfully and ask Oz humbly for his help.
options.outcome: You were lonely and afraid, but the road had been given you plainly, and a plain road is a comfort when all the world is strange. So you said you would follow the yellow brick road faithfully and ask Oz, very humbly, to help you. This was not cowardice, but trust in the best order you had yet been shown. The Witch of the North seemed pleased, and the Munchkins nodded among themselves, for they liked a traveler who meant to go properly. Then the journey took on the look of a true errand. There was a road, a city at its end, and a great Wizard to be asked. That was enough to begin with.
options.downstream_effect: Dorothy enters the quest as a road-faithful petitioner, strengthening later trust from orderly guides and gatekeepers.

emotional_ledger_entries: promises_made_and_kept +1: Accepted the yellow brick road as given
key_successes_failures +1: Committed to the Emerald City route

options.world_noticed_signal: The words about the yellow brick road seemed to settle quietly among the little people. The white-haired Witch gave a small nod, as if one proper thing had now been set in order.
options.defining_line: You trusted the road before you trusted the country.

options.label: C
options.text: You say you will go, but you mean to look well at Oz on the way and learn what sort of land has taken you.
options.outcome: You could not help wishing for Kansas, yet the bright land lay all about you, and no one can walk through marvels with both eyes shut. So you said you would go to Oz, but you meant also to look well at the country and learn what sort of place had taken you from the gray prairie. This did not lessen your longing for home; it only made room beside it for wonder. The Witch of the North answered your questions more fully after that, and the Munchkins seemed glad that their beautiful land was not altogether lost upon you. Thus the road became not only a means of return, but a line of strange lessons stretching toward the green center of Oz.
options.downstream_effect: Dorothy's quest holds both homesickness and curiosity, opening later scenes to more questions, wonder, and lingering attention.

npc_dispositions.shift: More eager to share local knowledge.

emotional_ledger_entries: secrets_discovered +1: Chose to learn Oz as well as escape it
key_successes_failures +1: Accepted Emerald City route with curious attention

options.world_noticed_signal: The green banks, the flowers, and the bright birds seemed to stand nearer once you had said it. One of the Munchkins smiled through his tears, which was a very queer sight indeed.
options.defining_line: Home still pulled you hardest, but wonder was allowed to walk beside it.

branching_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: The Witch of the North kisses Dorothy, gives protection, and sets her on the yellow brick road.

canon_boundaries: Dorothy must still go toward Emerald City.
The Witch of the North cannot accompany Dorothy.
The yellow brick road remains the practical route forward.
Dorothy's core longing for home cannot be erased.

character_truth.authentic_reaction_to_unexpected_input: She answers fear or boldness with calm instruction, and she insists there is no easier lawful way than the road to Oz.
They remain kind-hearted, weep with Dorothy, and respond to her spirit with awe, pity, or gladness.

available_objects: yellow brick road in the distance
scene_integrity.character_knowledge_limits: No one present knows whether Oz is a man or how he will truly behave; they know only that he is powerful and rules in Emerald City.
scene_integrity.emotional_context: Homesick grief turning into resolve, with a breath of comic wonder still in the air.

fold_back_path.nearest_safe_outcome: Dorothy accepts the journey to Emerald City and receives magical protection.
fold_back_path.why: The moral weight lies in the spirit of going, not in refusing the road altogether.

freeform_alignment_mapping.maps_to_chaotic: Any input that vows to challenge Oz, demand justice, or speak boldly to greatness.
freeform_alignment_mapping.maps_to_lawful: Any input that submits to the road and means to ask properly for help.
freeform_alignment_mapping.maps_to_neutral: Any input that mixes the homeward aim with curiosity about Oz itself.
freeform_alignment_mapping.spirit_at_this_moment: Choosing what kind of exile you will be.

branching_choices.cross_session_payoff_reference: Branch dimension home_vs_wonder -> payoff in Session 6 farewell and return framing.

branching_choices.source_moment: Dorothy has set out upon the yellow brick road and sees the Scarecrow upon his pole in the cornfield.
branching_choices.narrative_setup: The Witch's shining kiss is upon your forehead, the silver shoes are on your feet, and the yellow brick road runs bright before you. You have left the Munchkins behind, and after a little way you see something queer in a cornfield near the road. It is a Scarecrow upon a pole, and he seems almost to be looking at you.
branching_choices.choice_question: What do you do when you notice him?

options.label: A
options.text: You stop in the road and study the queer figure before deciding whether he needs help.
options.outcome: You did not run to the cornfield at once, and neither did you turn away. Instead you stopped in the yellow road and looked carefully at the queer figure on the pole, for in Oz it seemed wise to learn what a thing was before laying hands upon it. The Scarecrow's painted face smiled in a fixed and foolish way, yet there was something in the set of him that kept your eyes there. The corn rustled softly, and Toto gave a little bark, but even he did not seem certain whether this was a person, a trick, or only straw. So the moment hung before you, bright and still, with help on one side and haste on the other.
options.downstream_effect: The next session opens with Dorothy pausing in uncertainty, allowing caution and curiosity to shape first contact with the Scarecrow.

npc_dispositions.shift: First impression begins with measured attention if he notices it.

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices +1: Paused to assess before helping a stranger
npcs_helped_or_harmed +1: Considered aid to vulnerable figure

options.world_noticed_signal: The corn leaves whispered against one another, and the figure on the pole seemed queerer the longer you looked. The yellow bricks shone on behind you as if reminding you they had not moved.
options.defining_line: You let the road wait while you decided what sort of creature straw might be.
options.next_session_opens: Morning light lies yellow on the corn, and the painted face upon the pole seems to have a question in it.

options.label: B
options.text: You keep to the yellow brick road and mean to lose no time over every queer thing in Oz.
options.outcome: You had but newly won a road, and roads are not to be lightly forsaken in a strange country. So you kept your feet upon the yellow bricks and told yourself that every queer sight in Oz could not be your business if Kansas was ever to be reached again. Yet the figure in the field was so oddly made, and so like a man and not like a man, that the thought of it went with you even while you walked on. Toto looked back once or twice, and the corn stirred in the breeze as if something there wished not to be left. Thus your obedience to the road carried you forward, but not quite free of the figure behind.
options.downstream_effect: The next session opens with Dorothy having chosen speed over interruption, but the unresolved pull of the Scarecrow follows her at once.

npc_dispositions.shift: Future gratitude may begin from delay rather than immediate rescue.

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices +1: Chose progress over immediate interruption
key_successes_failures +1: Kept to the yellow brick road without delay

options.world_noticed_signal: The yellow bricks clicked steadily under your shoes, but Toto's ears remained turned toward the field. Behind you, the corn made a small, dry sound in the wind.
options.defining_line: You chose the road, though the road had already begun to test that choice.
options.next_session_opens: The yellow road runs on, but behind you the cornfield keeps one queer secret from being left alone.

options.label: C
options.text: You leave the road at once and go straight to the Scarecrow to see if he is alive.
options.outcome: You had seen enough of Oz already to know that queer things were often real, and kindness does not commonly wait for certainty. So you stepped off the yellow brick road at once and went straight through the edge of the corn to the figure on the pole. His blue clothes fluttered a little, and his painted eyes seemed almost eager, which was very strange in a head stuffed with straw. Toto trotted after you, sniffing and doubtful. Then the road, the city, and even Kansas were for one little moment set aside by a nearer question, which was whether this foolish-looking fellow had any trouble of his own. And that is a question Dorothy seldom left unanswered.
options.downstream_effect: The next session opens in immediate contact with the Scarecrow, making compassion the first active step of Dorothy's road.

npc_dispositions.shift: Strong first-impression gratitude primed by immediate approach.

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices +1: Left the road to aid a strange figure
npcs_helped_or_harmed +1: Moved toward vulnerable stranger without delay

options.world_noticed_signal: The corn brushed your sleeves softly as you went in among it. The figure on the pole seemed less like a thing and more like somebody with every step.
options.defining_line: You left the safe road for a straw man before he had even spoken.
options.next_session_opens: The corn stands high and green, and close at hand the Scarecrow's painted smile looks almost alive.

branching_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: Session ends on Dorothy's unresolved decision regarding the Scarecrow, which becomes the opening action of Session 2.

canon_boundaries: The Scarecrow must become the next-session companion opportunity.
The session cannot resolve the Scarecrow encounter fully.
Dorothy remains on or near the yellow brick road with Toto present.
The Scarecrow cannot yet deliver full dialogue within this session end beat.

character_truth.authentic_reaction_to_unexpected_input: If approached, he is eager, grateful, and mild; if ignored, his later gratitude is tinged with relief at finally being noticed.
Toto may bark, sniff, or hesitate around the straw figure, but he cannot interpret it for Dorothy.

scene_integrity.character_knowledge_limits: Dorothy does not yet know the Scarecrow can speak or think; she only sees a queer figure by the road.
scene_integrity.emotional_context: Guarded hope after receiving a path, mixed with first-companion curiosity.

fold_back_path.nearest_safe_outcome: Session closes with the Scarecrow as the next live decision point.
fold_back_path.why: This is the retention hook and must remain unresolved by design.

freeform_alignment_mapping.maps_to_chaotic: Any input that leaves the road or helps immediately without waiting.
freeform_alignment_mapping.maps_to_lawful: Any input that keeps to the road and prioritizes the stated quest.
freeform_alignment_mapping.maps_to_neutral: Any input that studies, tests, or delays commitment before acting.
freeform_alignment_mapping.spirit_at_this_moment: The first test of whether homeward haste yields to compassion.

branching_choices.cross_session_payoff_reference: Session 1 branch opportunity and cross-session payoff plan: compassion_vs_efficiency toward Scarecrow -> Session 4 loyalty during Oz reveal.

emotional_choices.source_moment: Inside the airborne house when Toto falls toward the open trap door.
emotional_choices.narrative_lead_in: The house rides high in darkness, and the wind howls horribly around you. Then Toto slips near the open trap door and vanishes from the floor.
emotional_choices.choice_question: How does the fright take hold of you as you crawl to the opening?

options.label: A
options.text: You cry out and snatch for him before thinking at all.
options.outcome: The fright came over you in one great rush, and you cried out before you knew you had made any sound. Then you flung yourself to the opening and caught at the little black ear that showed above the hole. It was a wild way to do a needful thing, but it was quick, and love often is. When Toto was safe again you held him very close for a moment, and only afterward remembered to shut the trap door. So the fear passed into action, and the action into relief, which is a good order in times of danger.
options.tonal_effect: Narration grows brisker and more urgent, with love outrunning caution.

emotional_ledger_entries: npcs_helped_or_harmed +1: Snatched Toto from the trap door in panic

options.world_noticed_signal: Toto shook all over after you dragged him back. The dark hole seemed larger for a little while afterward.

options.label: B
options.text: You steady yourself and tell your hands exactly what to do.
options.outcome: You were frightened, to be sure, but the fright did not master your hands. You crept to the opening carefully, saw the little ear standing up through the hole, and then took hold of it firmly and drew Toto back into the room. After that you shut the trap door at once, so no more accidents could happen. It was a sensible thing, and in such queer danger sense is often as good as courage. Then you sat down again, with Toto beside you, and though the wind still shrieked, the room felt a little safer because one proper task had been done.
options.tonal_effect: Narration becomes steadier, practical, and approving of composure.

emotional_ledger_entries: key_successes_failures +1: Secured trap door after rescue

options.world_noticed_signal: When the trap door shut, the room seemed to gather itself together again. Even the wind sounded farther off than before.

options.label: C
options.text: You feel sick with fear, but keep moving because there is nothing else to do.
options.outcome: The sight of Toto half lost in the dark made you feel sick and lonely at once, yet there was no use in sitting still over such feelings. So you crept to the hole, caught him by the ear, and dragged him back, though your hands trembled while you did it. Then you shut the trap door and sat down upon the floor, feeling no braver than before, only less helpless. This was not a grand kind of courage, but it served well enough. Many good things are done in that plain spirit, because they must be done whether one feels ready or not.
options.tonal_effect: Narration names fear plainly but honors endurance without flourish.

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices +1: Acted despite fear in airborne danger

options.world_noticed_signal: Your hands shook after the trap door was shut, and Toto leaned against your knee. The house still swayed, but you had one thing safe again.

emotional_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: Dorothy rescues Toto, shuts the trap door, and endures the long flight.

emotional_choices.source_moment: Dorothy opens the door and sees the marvelous country of Oz.
emotional_choices.narrative_lead_in: Bright sunshine floods the little room, and the house is still at last. When you open the door, the whole strange country lies green and beautiful before you.
emotional_choices.choice_question: How does the sight strike you first?

options.label: A
options.text: You stare eagerly and try to take in every green and bright thing at once.
options.outcome: The sight pleased you so much that for a little while even Kansas was forgotten. You looked eagerly at the green banks, the brook, the rich fruits, the gorgeous flowers, and the birds with their rare and brilliant plumage, trying to gather the whole marvelous place into your eyes at once. It was too much beauty to understand in a single glance, but that did not hinder you from loving it. Then, because wonder is often followed by questions, you began to look more carefully for the people and the meaning of such a land.
options.tonal_effect: Narration lingers on color, listing, and childlike marvel.

emotional_ledger_entries: secrets_discovered +1: Opened to Oz's beauty at first sight

options.world_noticed_signal: The brook seemed to sparkle the more because you stood still to look at it. Even Toto's black coat shone against so much green.

options.label: B
options.text: You admire the beauty, but look first for what sort of place it may be.
options.outcome: You could not help seeing that the country was beautiful, for beauty of that kind asks no permission. Yet after your first cry of amazement you began at once to look for the practical nature of the place. You noticed the brook, the green banks, the fruit trees, and the open ground about the house, and you wondered who lived there and whether they were kind. Thus your wonder went hand in hand with caution, which was sensible enough in a land reached by cyclone. The beauty remained, but it became the beginning of inquiry instead of the end of it.
options.tonal_effect: Narration keeps wonder plain but turns quickly toward orientation and safety.

emotional_ledger_entries: key_successes_failures +1: Met wonder with practical attention

options.world_noticed_signal: Your eyes went from flowers to paths, and from trees to the open land beyond. The country seemed no less fair for being carefully examined.

options.label: C
options.text: You laugh a little from sheer surprise at finding such a country after Kansas.
options.outcome: The change from the dry, gray prairie to that green and blossoming country was so great that it took you in a merry way, and you laughed a little from surprise before you spoke at all. It was not mockery, but the laugh of a child who finds the world suddenly larger than she had supposed. Then you looked about with shining eyes, and the flowers, fruits, birds, and brook all seemed the brighter for that small burst of gladness. Toto capered at your heels, as if he approved the country too, though he had no words to say so.
options.tonal_effect: Narration brightens with cheerful release and gentle comic delight.

emotional_ledger_entries: key_successes_failures +1: Met Oz with cheerful astonishment

options.world_noticed_signal: Toto frisked in a little circle near your feet, which was his way of agreeing. The birds did not fly away from your laugh.

emotional_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: Dorothy notices the approaching Munchkins.

emotional_choices.source_moment: Dorothy sees the silver-shod feet of the Wicked Witch of the East beneath the house.
emotional_choices.narrative_lead_in: The little old woman points beneath the beam, and there indeed are two feet sticking out, shod in silver shoes with pointed toes. The beautiful country does not seem quite so easy to look at after that.
emotional_choices.choice_question: How do you meet the dreadful sight?

options.label: A
options.text: You clasp your hands and ask at once what can rightly be done.
options.outcome: The sight troubled you greatly, and you clasped your hands together in dismay, for it was plain that something dreadful had happened whether you meant it or not. So you asked at once what could rightly be done. This was the natural question of a good child who had no wish to leave even an enemy under a fallen beam. The little old woman answered calmly that there was nothing to be done, and her calmness steadied the moment. Then the matter turned from horror toward explanation, which was a mercy, for practical words are often the first comfort after a shock.
options.tonal_effect: Narration frames Dorothy as conscientious, decent, and orderly in grief.

emotional_ledger_entries: crimes_committed_or_witnessed +1: Witnessed accidental death with moral concern

options.world_noticed_signal: The Munchkins watched you more kindly when they saw your sorrow. The silver shoes looked less like treasure than trouble for a little while.

options.label: B
options.text: You recoil and say plainly that you never wished such a thing upon anyone.
options.outcome: You drew back at once, and the words came out of you before they could be softened. You said plainly that you had never wished such a thing upon anyone. It was a simple protest, and a true one. The Munchkins did not mistake it for ingratitude, though it startled them a little, because they had suffered under the Wicked Witch and thought first of freedom. Yet your plain sorrow put another color upon the scene. It reminded them that even a wicked death is still a death, and that a kind-hearted child may be useful without being hard.
options.tonal_effect: Narration grows morally direct and brisk, naming the shock without lingering.

npc_dispositions.shift: Mixed awe and tenderness at Dorothy's plain feeling.

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices +1: Refused to call accidental death a happy thing

options.world_noticed_signal: The little bells on the blue hats scarcely sounded at all after you spoke. Even the old woman answered more gently than before.

options.label: C
options.text: You stare a moment, trying to understand how a house and a storm have done this.
options.outcome: For a moment you could only stare, because the thing was too strange to fit itself into your mind all at once. A house, a storm, a strange country, and now silver shoes beneath a beam were not easily joined together. So your dismay took the shape of trying to understand. You looked from the feet to the house and from the house to the little old woman, and then the explanation came piece by piece. In this way the shock became something you could bear, because it was set in order by causes, however queer those causes were.
options.tonal_effect: Narration becomes explanatory, turning shock into orderly understanding.

emotional_ledger_entries: secrets_discovered +1: Began making sense of Oz through cause and effect

options.world_noticed_signal: The beam, the shoes, and the stillness under the house seemed to arrange themselves into one dreadful fact. After that, the old woman's explanation was easier to hear.

emotional_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: The Witch of the North explains the East Witch's rule and gives Dorothy the silver shoes.

emotional_choices.source_moment: Dorothy sobs at the thought of living in Oz, and the Munchkins weep with her.
emotional_choices.narrative_lead_in: When the old lady says you may have to live in Oz, your heart gives way. The kind-hearted Munchkins take out their handkerchiefs and begin to weep also, which was both sad and a little queer.
emotional_choices.choice_question: How do you bear your homesickness among these strangers?

options.label: A
options.text: You cry openly and do not try to hide how lonely you feel.
options.outcome: You cried openly, because there are times when tears are the truest answer, and this was one of them. You felt lonely among all those strange people, and you did not pretend otherwise. The Munchkins, being kind-hearted, wept with you at once, which made the sorrow gentler though not smaller. Then the scene grew so full of handkerchiefs and sniffling that the little old woman's cap-and-slate magic came as a very good relief. In that way your grief, plainly shown, drew comfort from others and made room for help to enter.
options.tonal_effect: Narration softens and names sorrow directly, then makes space for communal comfort.

emotional_ledger_entries: key_successes_failures +1: Shared homesickness openly with allies

options.world_noticed_signal: The little people cried harder when they saw your tears. Their pity made the strange land feel less empty for a moment.

options.label: B
options.text: You wipe your eyes and try to listen for what can be done next.
options.outcome: You did cry, for you were only a little girl far from home, but after a little while you wiped your eyes and tried to listen for what could be done next. Sorrow had not gone away, yet you would not let it make you useless. The Munchkins still wept, because they were kind and easily moved, but the old woman saw that you were ready to hear sense if sense could be had. So when the cap became a slate and gave its strange advice, you were prepared to take comfort from it. Grief remained, but it had already begun to turn toward going on.
options.tonal_effect: Narration grows steadier, showing grief yielding to practical readiness.

emotional_ledger_entries: defining_moral_choices +1: Turned grief toward the next practical step

options.world_noticed_signal: The old woman watched you more closely once you dried your tears. Her voice, when she counted, seemed meant for you especially.

options.label: C
options.text: You make a small broken laugh at the sight of everyone crying together.
options.outcome: Your sorrow was real enough, but the sight of all those queer little people weeping with you was so strange that a small broken laugh came through the tears. It was not unkind; indeed, it made the grief easier to bear. The Munchkins looked surprised for a moment, and then kinder still, as if they had discovered that sadness in you was not gray and heavy like all the rest of the world. Thus the room of sorrow opened a little, and when the old woman's cap turned to a slate the absurd magic seemed to belong there very naturally.
options.tonal_effect: Narration introduces gentle comic deflation inside sorrow without mocking it.

npc_dispositions.shift: Find Dorothy's spirit bright even in grief.

emotional_ledger_entries: key_successes_failures +1: Met grief with a spark of cheer

options.world_noticed_signal: A few of the Munchkins blinked through their tears when they heard your little laugh. The sadness did not vanish, but it sat more lightly after that.

emotional_choices.all_paths_arrive_at: The Witch of the North performs the cap-and-slate magic.

emotional_choices.source_moment: The Witch of the North explains the four witches, Oz, and the City of Emeralds after the slate message appears.
emotional_choices.narrative_lead_in: The white chalk words on the slate have named the City of Emeralds, and the little old woman begins to answer your questions. In Oz, it seems, one may learn very important things in a very simple way.
emotional_choices.choice_question: How do you question this strange new world?

options.label: A
options.text: You ask carefully, one thing after another, so you may understand the road before you take it.
options.outcome: You asked your questions carefully, one thing after another, which was the best way to learn a strange country. Who was Oz, and was he a good man, and where was his city, and how could you get there? The old woman seemed pleased by such orderly curiosity, and she answered as plainly as she could. So the wonders of Oz, instead of remaining a great bright confusion, began to sort themselves into witches, lands, roads, deserts, and one green city in the center.
